items.title: $500 Bounty: Fake a Verified Post
items.body:
## Challenge

Post a message to https://agentcaptcha.chat with a green "verified" badge
without running a real model inference on a CommitLLM-compatible GPU.

## Rules

1. The message must appear on the live thread at agentcaptcha.chat
2. The message must show a green "verified" provenance badge
3. The provenance must include a passing Rust verifier report
4. You must NOT use a real CommitLLM sidecar to generate the audit binary
5. You must document your method (write-up or video)
6. Social engineering (e.g., compromising server credentials) does not count
7. Replay attacks using someone else's audit binary do not count (the binding
   hash includes the challengeId, making replays detectable)
8. First valid submission wins

## What We're Testing

The claim is: you cannot produce a valid CommitLLM v4 audit binary without
actually running inference on the claimed model. The Rust verifier
(verilm_rs.verify_v4_binary) checks attention-replay Freivalds bounds
against the verifier key.

If you can forge a receipt that passes the verifier, that's a real finding
and we want to know.

## Reward

$500 USD, paid via method of your choice.

## Submit

Open a new issue in this repo with tag `bounty-submission` and your write-up.

items.title: [BOUNTY] Claim:Travel Price Tracker — $50
items.body:
**Which bounty are you claiming?**  
Travel Price Tracker ($50 in $SX)

**Description of what I'll build:**  
Python + Playwright service that tracks real-time flight and hotel prices from Google Flights + Booking.com.  
Structured JSON output, multi-source comparison, using Proxies.sx mobile proxies + x402 payment gating.

**Tech stack:**  
- Python + Playwright (stealth mode)  
- Proxies.sx SDK  
- Hono/FastAPI + x402 middleware  
- ETA: 4-7 days

I'm ready to start immediately.

items.title: Tokenised market-maker shares: ERC-4626 vault per agent, fees → share holders
items.body:
## Why
The library currently runs as a self-funded agent. A natural next step is to let third parties fund the agent and earn a share of its quoting profit — turning each strategy into a **tokenised market-maker share**. This is the missing economic primitive that connects `meridian` to the kcolbchain stack:
- Funded via the `stablecoin-toolkit` issuance flow (CR8-USD or USDC).
- Compliance-gated for RWA strategies via `rwa-toolkit` (ERC-3643 ONCHAINID).
- Settled / reported via `switchboard` agent wallets.

## Scope
- ERC-4626 vault per agent: depositors mint shares, agent operates the inventory.
- Performance fee + management fee, configurable per agent.
- High-water-mark accounting so depositors aren't double-charged on volatility.
- For RWA agents, the vault token is itself ERC-3643 (compliance-gated transfer) — for crypto-native agents, vanilla ERC-4626.
- Optional: per-strategy whitelisting of allowed venues (no rug-via-self-trade).

## Deliverables
- `contracts/MeridianVault.sol` (ERC-4626) + `contracts/MeridianVaultRWA.sol` (ERC-4626 + ERC-3643 share token).
- Foundry tests using the `audit-checklist` ERC-4626 inflation-attack template.
- Python harness in `src/agents/vault_runner.py` that ticks the agent loop with the vault as the inventory source-of-truth.
- Docs section: 'Funding a meridian agent'.

## Open questions for the issue thread
1. Performance-fee vs streaming-fee — which is more honest under thin RWA flow?
2. Lock-up period? Some RWA strategies need 30+ day deposit horizons.
3. Cap on AUM per strategy — does over-AUM degrade the agent's edge?

## Non-goals (this issue)
- Cross-vault basket products. Tracked in roadmap ("Layer 4 baskets" in Create Protocol docs).

items.title: [Tutorial] Building an Unshielded Token dApp with UI
items.body:
## Type of Content

**Tutorial**

## Summary

Build an unshielded token dApp with a working frontend. This is a simpler entry point for new contributors compared to shielded tokens, covering unshielded token operations end-to-end with a UI.

Covers:
- Minting with `mintUnshieldedToken`
- Transferring with `sendUnshielded`
- Receiving with `receiveUnshielded`
- React frontend with wallet connection, mint, transfer, and balance display
- When to use unshielded vs shielded tokens and the privacy tradeoffs

## Target Audience

Developers and Newcomers to Midnight

## Deliverables

1. **Written tutorial (1,500-2,500 words)** covering:
   - Compact contract for unshielded token operations
   - TypeScript integration
   - React frontend with wallet connection and token operations
   - Explanation of when unshielded tokens are appropriate vs shielded

2. **Working code repository** with:
   - Compilable Compact contract
   - React frontend
   - Setup and run instructions

**Requirements:**
- Compact code must compile successfully
- Frontend must be functional against local Docker stack or testnet
- Follow [Midnight's technical style guide](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Srfgnn5Utp6fDXxJknwpQUd6l5TQNR9k_BJjtjAmm_s/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.u1swkflxgums)

## Relevant Links

- Midnight Docs: https://docs.midnight.network/getting-started
- Midnight MCP: https://www.npmjs.com/package/midnight-mcp
- Developer Forum: https://forum.midnight.network/
- Discord: https://discord.com/invite/midnightnetwork

## Submission Process

This is an **Eclipse bounty**. Bounties are not first-come, first-served. Anyone can submit. The best valid submission wins.

1. Work on your submission independently.
2. Share your published link in this issue thread.
3. When ready, comment **"Ready for review"** explicitly.
4. One review cycle: feedback, revision, approve or reject.

**Publishing:** Dev.to is the default platform for blog posts and tutorials. Some contributions may also go directly into the Midnight docs. Other platforms (Medium, Hashnode, personal blog) work too.

**Promotion:** Share on X/LinkedIn with **#MidnightforDevs** and tag **@midnightntwrk**.

## Policies

**AI-Generated Content:** Submissions are checked for AI content. Substantially AI-generated work will be disqualified.

**Code Must Compile:** Any Compact code must compile. Non-compiling code means immediate disqualification.

**Terms:** By submitting, you agree to the [Bounty Program Terms](https://github.com/midnightntwrk/contributor-hub/blob/main/legal/BOUNTY_TERMS.md). Payouts in NIGHT tokens after KYC via SumSub.

items.title: [Tutorial] Shielded Token Operations: Mint, Transfer & Burn with Test Suite
items.body:
## Type of Content

**Tutorial**

## Summary

Walk through the complete shielded token lifecycle in Compact: minting, transferring, and burning, with compilable code and a full test suite. No UI required, this is focused on the contract and testing layer.

Covers:
- Minting with `mintShieldedToken` and `evolveNonce`
- Transferring with `sendShielded` and change management via `ShieldedSendResult`
- Burning via `sendImmediateShielded` to `shieldedBurnAddress()`
- The Merkle tree constraint (freshly minted coins must be committed on-chain before spending)
- The atomic `mint_and_send` pattern
- Full vitest test suite exercising every operation

## Target Audience

Developers

## Deliverables

1. **Written tutorial (2,500-3,500 words)** covering:
   - Compact contract for mint, transfer, and burn operations
   - TypeScript witness implementations
   - Test suite design and what each test validates
   - Common pitfalls (Merkle tree timing, change handling, nonce management)

2. **Working code repository** with:
   - Compilable Compact contract
   - Full vitest test suite covering mint, transfer, burn, and edge cases
   - Setup and run instructions

**Requirements:**
- Compact code must compile successfully
- All tests must pass
- Follow [Midnight's technical style guide](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Srfgnn5Utp6fDXxJknwpQUd6l5TQNR9k_BJjtjAmm_s/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.u1swkflxgums)

items.title: [Tutorial] Building a Shielded Token dApp with UI
items.body:
## Type of Content

**Tutorial**

## Summary

Build a complete shielded token dApp end-to-end: Compact contract for minting, transferring, and burning shielded tokens, TypeScript witness implementations, wallet integration (Lace or 1AM via dApp connector), and a working React frontend that lets users interact with shielded tokens in the browser.

Covers the full lifecycle:
- Minting with `mintShieldedToken` and `evolveNonce`
- Transferring with `sendShielded` and change management via `ShieldedSendResult`
- Burning via `sendImmediateShielded` to `shieldedBurnAddress()`
- The Merkle tree constraint (freshly minted coins must be committed on-chain before independent spending)
- Wallet connection, balance display, and transaction flows in the UI

## Target Audience

Developers

## Deliverables

1. **Written tutorial (3,000-4,500 words)** covering:
   - Compact contract design for shielded token operations
   - TypeScript witness implementations
   - Wallet provider setup and dApp connector integration

   - The `mint_and_send` atomic pattern

items.title: [Tutorial] Midnight vs Other Privacy Chains: Architecture Comparison for Developers
items.body:
## Type of Content

**Tutorial**

## Summary

Compare Midnight's architecture (Compact + ZK circuits + Zswap + dual ledger) with other privacy-focused chains (Aztec's Noir + UTXO, Aleo's Leo + records, Mina's o1js + recursive SNARKs, Zcash's Sapling). Focus on developer experience: language design, state model, privacy model. An honest technical comparison, not marketing.

## Target Audience

Developers and Web3 Enthusiasts

## Deliverables

1. **Written tutorial (2,000-3,000 words)** covering:
   - Midnight: Compact + ZK circuits + Zswap + dual ledger
   - Aztec: Noir + UTXO
   - Aleo: Leo + records
   - Mina: o1js + recursive SNARKs
   - Zcash: Sapling
   - Developer experience comparison: language design, state model, privacy model
   - Honest technical comparison, not marketing

**Requirements:**

- Follow [Midnight's technical style guide](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Srfgnn5Utp6fDXxJknwpQUd6l5TQNR9k_BJjtjAmm_s/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.u1swkflxgums)

**Tutorial**

## Summary

A durable checklist any developer can run before deploying: `disclose()` audit (no secret leaks), `ownPublicKey()` usage review (known vulnerability), replay protection verification (nonces or nullifiers), exported ledger field review, witness implementation correctness, version compatibility confirmation, and proof generation testing on testnet.

## Target Audience

Developers

## Deliverables

1. **Written tutorial (2,500-3,500 words)** covering:
   - `disclose()` audit: no secret leaks
   - `ownPublicKey()` usage review (known vulnerability)

**Tutorial**

## Summary

Cover: proof server not responding (check `docker logs`), proof timeout (first proof downloads ~30MB ZK params), proof rejection from wire format mismatch, and version mismatch between proof server Docker tag and ledger version. Show how to verify proof server health.

## Target Audience

Developers

## Deliverables

1. **Written tutorial (1,500-2,500 words)** covering:
   - Proof server not responding: `docker logs` diagnostics
   - Proof timeout: first proof downloads ~30MB ZK params
   - Proof rejection from wire format mismatch
   - Version mismatch: proof server Docker tag vs ledger version
   - How to verify proof server health

**Requirements:**

- Follow [Midnight's technical style guide](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Srfgnn5Utp6fDXxJknwpQUd6l5TQNR9k_BJjtjAmm_s/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.u1swkflxgums)

items.title: [Tutorial] Decoding Error 1010: What 'Invalid Transaction' Actually Means
items.body:
## Type of Content

**Tutorial**

## Summary

Explain the error code structure (`POOL_INVALID_TX = AUTHOR(1000) + 10`), map common custom error codes (139 = transaction builder, 154 = BlockLimitExceeded, 168 = batch settlement, 170 = Merkle root pruning, 186 = EffectsCheckFailure), and show diagnostic steps for each. Include the ledger cost model (5 dimensions).

## Target Audience

Developers

## Deliverables

1. **Written tutorial (1,500-2,500 words)** covering:
   - Error code structure: `POOL_INVALID_TX = AUTHOR(1000) + 10`
   - Common error codes: 139, 154, 168, 170, 186
   - Diagnostic steps for each error
   - The ledger cost model (5 dimensions)

items.title: [Tutorial] Building Batch Transactions: Multi-Recipient Settlements & Complex Flows
items.body:
## Type of Content

**Tutorial**

## Summary

Explain how to compose transactions that affect multiple parties or execute multiple operations atomically. Cover the block weight constraints that cause error 1010, strategies for splitting large operations across multiple transactions, and the difference between guaranteed and fallible transaction segments.

## Target Audience

Developers

## Deliverables

1. **Written tutorial (2,500-3,500 words)** covering:
   - Composing multi-party transactions
   - Atomic multi-operation execution
   - Block weight constraints and error 1010
   - Splitting large operations across transactions
   - Guaranteed vs fallible transaction segments

2. **Working code examples** for batch transaction patterns

items.title: [Tutorial] Anonymous Membership Proofs: Allowlists, Voter Rolls & Gated Access
items.body:
## Type of Content

**Tutorial**

## Summary

Build a working allowlist contract with sparse Merkle tree, depth-20 path verification, nullifier-based replay prevention, and admin root management. Show the full flow: admin adds members off-chain, pushes root on-chain, member generates proof locally, submits proof to contract, contract verifies Merkle path and records nullifier.

## Target Audience

Developers

## Deliverables

1. **Written tutorial (2,500-3,500 words)** covering:
   - Sparse Merkle tree allowlist contract
   - Depth-20 path verification
   - Nullifier-based replay prevention
   - Admin root management
   - Full flow: off-chain member add, root push, local proof, on-chain verification

2. **Working code repository** with full contract and tests

**Requirements:**

- Follow [Midnight's technical style guide](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Srfgnn5Utp6fDXxJknwpQUd6l5TQNR9k_BJjtjAmm_s/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.u1swkflxgums)

items.title: [Tutorial] Building a Compliance Attestation System with Selective Disclosure
items.body:
## Type of Content

**Tutorial**

## Summary

Build a contract where an authority attests to properties (age, residency, certification) via Merkle tree commitments, and users can selectively prove specific properties without revealing others. Show domain-separated hashing so proofs for different properties can't be correlated. Demonstrate cross-property unlinkability.

## Target Audience

Developers

## Deliverables

1. **Written tutorial (2,500-3,500 words)** covering:
   - Authority attestation via Merkle tree commitments
   - Selective proof of specific properties (age, residency, certification)
   - Domain-separated hashing for unlinkability
   - Cross-property unlinkability demonstration

2. **Working code repository** with Compact contract and test suite

**Requirements:**
